title: Witch Week - Chapter 9 (Fragment 3)
text:
“Get back to your seat,” Mr. Wentworth said, and let go of Charles with a sharp push.

Charles felt his way through swimming colored blurs, down to the other end of the old lab. Those crooked white squares must be the book and the old exam paper. But his pen, he remembered, had fallen on the floor. How was he to find it, in this state, let alone write with it?

“What are you standing there for?” Mr. Wentworth barked at him. “Put your glasses on and get back to work!”

Charles jumped with terror. He found himself diving for his seat, and hooking his glasses on as he dived. The world clicked into focus. He saw his pen lying almost under his feet and bent to pick it up. But surely, he thought, as he was half under the bench, his glasses had been in two pieces? He had heard a dreadfully final snapping noise. He thought he had felt them come apart. He put his hand up hurriedly and felt his glasses—there was no point taking them off and looking, because then he would not be able to see. They felt all right. Entire and whole. Either he had made a mistake, or the plastic had snapped and not the metal inside. Much relieved, Charles sat up with the pen in his hand.

And stared at what it had written by itself. I am Watts down scrum Minor ripping this fun afternoon. I fun Minor am half this afternoon Watts . . . and so on, for two whole pages. It was no good. Mr. Towers was bound to notice. Charles sighed and began writing. Perhaps he should stop doing witchcraft. Nothing seemed to go right.

Consequently, the rest of the evening was rather quiet. Charles sat in devvy running his thumb over the fat cushion of blister on his finger, not wanting to give up witchcraft and knowing he dared not go on. He felt such a mixture of regret and terror that it quite bewildered him. Simon was subdued too. Brian Wentworth was back, sitting scribbling industriously, with one eye still turned slightly inward, but Simon seemed to have lost his desire to hit Brian for the time being. And Simon’s friends followed Simon’s lead.

Nan kept quiet also, because of what Nirupam had said, but, however hard she reasoned with herself, she could not get rid of that bubbling inner confidence. It was still with her in the dormitory that night. It stayed, in spite of Delia, Deborah, Heather, and the rest, who began on her in their usual way.

“It was a bit much, that spell on Simon!”

“Really, Nan, I know we asked you, but you should think first.”

“Look what he did to Theresa. And she lost her knitting over it.”

And Nan, instead of submitting or apologizing, as she usually did, said, “What’s put it into your pretty little heads that that spell was mine?”

“Because we know you’re a witch,” said Heather.

“Of course,” said Nan. “But what gave you the idea I was the only one? You think, Heather, instead of just opening your little pink mouth and letting words trickle out. I told you, it takes time to make a spell. I told you about picking herbs and flying around and chanting, didn’t I? And I left out the way you have to catch bats. That takes ages, even on a fast modern broomstick, because bats are so good at dodging. And you were with me in the bathroom, and with me all the time all this last week, and you know I haven’t had time to catch bats or pick herbs, and you’ve seen I haven’t been muttering and incantating. So you see? It wasn’t me.”

She could tell they were convinced, because they all looked so disappointed. Heather muttered, “And you said you couldn’t fly that broomstick!” but she said nothing more. Nan was pleased. She seemed to have shut them up without losing her reputation as a witch.

vocabulary.definition: Express regret for something done wrong.
vocabulary.example:
”

And Nan, instead of submitting or apologizing, as she usually did, said, “What’s put it into your pretty little heads that that spell was mine?

vocabulary.definition: To speak or shout abruptly or aggressively.
vocabulary.example: Wentworth barked at him.

vocabulary.definition: Confused or puzzled completely.
vocabulary.example: He felt such a mixture of regret and terror that it quite bewildered him.

vocabulary.definition: The handle of a broom, often imagined as a flying device in witchcraft.
vocabulary.example: That takes ages, even on a fast modern broomstick, because bats are so good at dodging.

vocabulary.definition: A feeling of self-assurance arising from one's abilities.
vocabulary.example: Nan kept quiet also, because of what Nirupam had said, but, however hard she reasoned with herself, she could not get rid of that bubbling inner confidence.

vocabulary.definition: As a result or effect of something.
vocabulary.example: Consequently, the rest of the evening was rather quiet.

vocabulary.definition: Avoid (someone or something) by a sudden quick movement.
vocabulary.example: That takes ages, even on a fast modern broomstick, because bats are so good at dodging.

vocabulary.definition: A large bedroom for a number of people in a school or institution.
vocabulary.example: It was still with her in the dormitory that night.

vocabulary.definition: In a very bad or unpleasant manner.
vocabulary.example: He had heard a dreadfully final snapping noise.

vocabulary.definition: Coming at the end of a series.
vocabulary.example: He had heard a dreadfully final snapping noise.

vocabulary.definition: Plants used for flavoring, medicine, or perfume.
vocabulary.example: I told you about picking herbs and flying around and chanting, didn’t I?

vocabulary.definition: In a rushed or hasty manner.
vocabulary.example: He put his hand up hurriedly and felt his glasses—there was no point taking them off and looking, because then he would not be able to see.

vocabulary.definition: To chant or speak magical spells.
vocabulary.example: And you were with me in the bathroom, and with me all the time all this last week, and you know I haven’t had time to catch bats or pick herbs, and you’ve seen I haven’t been muttering and incantating.

vocabulary.definition: In a hardworking and diligent manner.
vocabulary.example: Brian Wentworth was back, sitting scribbling industriously, with one eye still turned slightly inward, but Simon seemed to have lost his desire to hit Brian for the time being.

vocabulary.definition: Speak quietly and indistinctly.
vocabulary.example: And you were with me in the bathroom, and with me all the time all this last week, and you know I haven’t had time to catch bats or pick herbs, and you’ve seen I haven’t been muttering and incantating.

vocabulary.definition: Making little or no noise.
vocabulary.example: Consequently, the rest of the evening was rather quiet.

vocabulary.definition: Think, understand, and form judgments logically.
vocabulary.example: Nan kept quiet also, because of what Nirupam had said, but, however hard she reasoned with herself, she could not get rid of that bubbling inner confidence.

vocabulary.definition: No longer feeling distressed or anxious; reassured.
vocabulary.example: Much relieved, Charles sat up with the pen in his hand.

vocabulary.definition: The beliefs or opinions generally held about someone or something.
vocabulary.example: She seemed to have shut them up without losing her reputation as a witch.

vocabulary.definition: Write or draw something hurriedly or carelessly.
vocabulary.example: Brian Wentworth was back, sitting scribbling industriously, with one eye still turned slightly inward, but Simon seemed to have lost his desire to hit Brian for the time being.

vocabulary.definition: A form of words supposed to have magical power.
vocabulary.example: “It was a bit much, that spell on Simon!

vocabulary.definition: Quiet and rather reflective or depressed.
vocabulary.example: Simon was subdued too.

vocabulary.definition: Accept or yield to a superior force or the authority of another.
vocabulary.example:
”

And Nan, instead of submitting or apologizing, as she usually did, said, “What’s put it into your pretty little heads that that spell was mine?

vocabulary.definition: The practice of magic by witches.
vocabulary.example: Perhaps he should stop doing witchcraft.
